coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for sensing metal ions and small molecules

coredata.dc:creator: XU, Xiang-Jun
GE, Sheng
LI, Dong-Qin
XU, Zi-Qiang
WANG, Er-Jing
WANG, Shi-Min
coredata.dc:description: As one kind of new carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have excellent optoelectric properties and indicate widespread applications in sensing, display, energy storage and catalysis. Although the precise structure of CDs needs to be further analyzed and investigated, it is generally accepted that there are large amounts of electron-rich groups and substituents on the surface of CDs, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, thiol, and heterocycles, depending on the precursors used and preparation route. Therefore, the inherent advantages of electron-rich CDs make them very suitable for sensing specific substances including metal ions and electron-deficient small molecules. The sensing performance of CDs can be further improved through physical or chemical surface modification. The recent progress in CDs application for sensing metal ions and small functional molecules in recent years is specifically summarized and the sensing mechanism behind that was elaborated. Finally, the research prospective and performance improvement for CDs are addressed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence sensors
Metal ions
Small molecules
Sensing mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent CDs-based sensors for metal ions
CDs-based sensors for Hg2+ and Ag+ ions
Fluorescent CDs-based sensors for bio-active small molecules
CDs-based sensors for glucose and other small bioactive molecules

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52073082, 51603063 and 22073025) and Overseas Expertise Introduction Project for Discipline Innovation (D18025). We also thanked Prof. Yanyan Fu of Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, for constructible discussion on the sensing mechanism.

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52073082 , 51603063 and 22073025 ) and Overseas Expertise Introduction Project for Discipline Innovation ( D18025 ). We also thanked Prof. Yanyan Fu of Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, for constructible discussion on the sensing mechanism.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for sensing based on PET process.
Schematic diagram for sensing based on FRET process.
Schematic diagram for sensing based on ICT process.

(A) Scheme showing the formation of CD‒Fe complex and as a result quenching the emission by CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, Fe3+, and CDs-Fe [inset is Photograph of CDs (left) and CDs_Fe (right)], (C) The linear calibration plot for Fe3+ detection in human blood serum. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [25] . Copyright©2020 Springer Nature Limited.
Schematic diagram of detecting Ag+ and Cys-based on y-CDs dual function. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [63]. Copyright©2015 American Chemical Society.

(Left) Visualized detection of trinitrophenol (TNP) over other nitro-based explosives using CDs, and (right) HOMO-LUMO energy levels of CDs, TNP, TATB, HMX, TNT and RDX, respectively. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [91]. Copyright©2018 American Chemical Society.
Synthesis of other CDs and their sensing performance for Fe3+.
Synthesis of other CDs and their performance for Cu2+sensing.
Fluorescent on-off-on CDs-based bifunctional sensors for metal ions and GSH.
Synthesis of some CDs and their performance for sensing small bioactive molecules.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent carbon dots for sensing metal ions and small molecules

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aMinistry-of-Education Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University, No. 368 Youyi Avenue, Wuchang District, Wuhan 430062, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Key Laboratory of Crop Genetic Improvement

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNational Key Laboratory of Crop Genetic Improvement, Huazhong Agricultural University, No.1 Shizishan Street, Hongshan District, Wuhan 430070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one kind of new carbon nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have excellent optoelectric properties and indicate widespread applications in sensing, display, energy storage and catalysis. Although the precise structure of CDs needs to be further analyzed and investigated, it is generally accepted that there are large amounts of electron-rich groups and substituents on the surface of CDs, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, thiol, and heterocycles, depending on the precursors used and preparation route. Therefore, the inherent advantages of electron-rich CDs make them very suitable for sensing specific substances including metal ions and electron-deficient small molecules. The sensing performance of CDs can be further improved through physical or chemical surface modification. The recent progress in CDs application for sensing metal ions and small functional molecules in recent years is specifically summarized and the sensing mechanism behind that was elaborated. Finally, the research prospective and performance improvement for CDs are addressed.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are generally regarded as dispersible sphere-like fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with size less than 10 nm. In 2004, Xu et al. discovered and separated fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with size about 1 nm. They claimed the discovered carbon nanoparticles to be interesting nanomaterials in their own right [1]. In following 2006, Prof. Sun synthesized quantum-sized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles through laser ablation of a carbon target and defined the carbon nanoparticles as CDs for the first time [2]. After surface passivation with polyethylene glycol (PEG) and poly(propionylethyleneimine-polyethyleneimine) (PPEI-EI) polymers, these CDs exhibited stable and diverse fluorescence from blue, green, till to red. After that, the research on CDs has been becoming a hot topic. Compared with inorganic semiconductor quantum dots, CDs possess the merits of low or no toxicity, good water solubility, simple synthesis, low cost, tunable multi-color fluorescence, excellent stability and good biocompatibility, and can be widely used in sensing, catalysis, energy conversion, display [3], etc. CDs can be prepared from various raw materials including inorganic precursors, organic precursors, and biomass materials via top-down or bottom-up process. The inorganic precursors are mainly carbon-based materials such as activated carbon [4], graphene [5], carbon nanotubes [6] and soot [7]. Organic precursors cover small organic molecules [8] containing carboxyl, hydroxyl, or amino moieties, amino acids [9], saccharides [10], and chain-type polymers [11]. These organic precursors undergo intermolecular/intramolecular dehydration, rearrangement, polymerization, aromatization and carbonization to form CDs. Biomass materials are mainly from the nature such as cow milk [12], egg white [13], fruit peel [14], coffee residue [15] and peanut hull [16], and are a preferable choice for CDs preparation.

One typical characteristic of CDs is their tunable emission dependence on excitation wavelength and particle size, which are beneficial to device simplification and interference elimination of background fluorescence. Generally, surface passivation is necessary for alleviating agglomeration and improving emission stability. Although, various kinds of CDs with diverse fluorescence properties have been developed, the emission mechanism of CDs is intricate and has not yet been clearly elaborated. Till now, surface defect-derived origins, quantum size effect, fluorescent groups and carbon core are regarded as the main source for CDs emission [17]. In the theory of surface defect-derived origins, it is regarded that there are some sites with non-perfect sp2 domains to form surface energy traps. These sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon and other functionalized electronic state defects are beneficial to the radiative recombination and multicolor emission under different excitation [18]. Higher surface oxidation degree or other effective modification would produce more surface defects, inducing red-shift emission. As for the theory of quantum size effect, the fluorescence properties of CDs are influenced by the particle size, that is, the energy gap of CDs is determined by the size of the conjugated sp2 π domains. For example, Kang [19] et al. prepared 1.2‒3.8 nm CDs using an electrochemical method. Smaller CDs (1.2 nm) emitted violet light with wavelength about 350 nm, and medium-size CDs (1.5‒3 nm) showed fluorescence in the range of 400‒700 nm, while the emission of the larger CDs (3.8 nm) shifted to the near IR region. As for the last mechanism, the fluorescence is regarded to be from functional fluorescent segments. The fluorescent center is composed of organic fluorophores, which are connected to the exterior or interior of carbon chains for direct light emission. Carbonization of small molecule precursors can form fluorophores in CDs forms. With temperature increase, the CDs would undergo further carbonization and the molecular fluorophores converted into carbon core. Just due to the rich fluorescence properties of CDs, researches can utilize rich precursors to synthesize diverse CDs with prominent fluorescence behaviors to be used in different occasions.

As a new fluorescent nanomaterial, an interesting application of CDs is their fluorescence-based sensing. The phenomena of fluorescence quenching, fluorescence enhancement and fluorescence spectrum shift are usually utilized to furnish the fluorescent sensing. A typical fluorescent sensor is composed of a recognizing part for specific combination with the analyte and a sensing part for exporting a fluorescence signal. In the Review, the progresses of CDs as chemosensors for metal ions, bioactive small molecules, and nitroaromatic explosives in recent years are briefly summarized. In view of the numerous published papers concerning the sensing performance of CDs, we could not cover all the related research, and only typical and representative examples are cited for reference and the sensing mechanism is also discussed. Finally, critical challenges that influence the performance improvement and perspectives of CDs for real-world application are addressed. Hopefully, this article could provide a comprehensive overview of the current status of CDs sensor research and open new perspectives toward the development of CDs with excellent sensing performance.
For application of CDs in fluorescent chemosensor, there are mainly four photophysical and photochemical processes are involved [20].

A photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process is commonly used in the design of fluorescent sensors and fluorescent switches. PET usually occurs between a ground-state molecule (electron provider) and an excited-state fluorescent molecule (electron acceptor). After excitation of the fluorescent molecule, the electron on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) of the electron provider will transfer to the HOMO of the fluorescent molecule while the electron on the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the fluorescent molecule will jump to the HOMO of the electron provider. This process will result in the fluorescence quenching of the electron acceptor (fluorescent molecule). When a third species as guest exists, the HOMO energy level of the electron provider becomes approximate to or lower than the that of the fluorescent molecule, the PET process will be depressed or precluded, and the fluorescence of the electron acceptor will be recovered or be enhanced (Chart 1 ). For CDs-based system, owing to rich electron-donating groups on the surface, the energy levels of CDs make them more suitable to be used as the electron acceptor after excitation. The excitation-state electron of CDs would them jump to the electron provider, completing PET process and realizing sensing.

The occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) requires two fluorophore molecules: one acts as the donor (D) and the other as the acceptor (A). It is demanded that the fluorescence spectrum of the donor have good overlapping with the absorption spectrum of the acceptor. After excitation of the donor, the excitation energy of the donor would be transferred to the acceptor, usually inducing the fluorescence emission of the latter accompanied by the fluorescence quenching of the donor (Chart 2 ). The energy transfer rate and quantum yield (QY) are also related with the cn, dipole orientation, and distance between the two fluorophores, solvent property, and the bridge structure connecting the donor and acceptor.

Inner filter effect (IFE) is an important path for nonradiative energy conversion. In this model, there is certain overlapping of the absorption spectrum of an absorber with the excitation and/or emission spectrum of a fluorogen, the excitation intensity and/or emission intensity of the fluorogen deceased or totally diminished. The good matching between the absorber and the fluorogen is an important factor to construct an efficient sensor for target detection. There are mainly three IFE-based sensing modes: turn-off, turn-on, and ratiometric fluorescence assay. Generally, a sensing target would first interact with the exterior of CDs to form a new "shell" layer, and the new shell layer then would influence the excitation and/or emission property of the interior of CDs through IFE, thus realizing sensing of the target.

The process of intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) is a photophysical one also widely utilized in chemosensors and molecular switches. Compared with PET system, an ICT molecule has a conjugated structure containing electron-pushing (D) and electron-pulling (A) moieties at two ends. When an analyte is combined or interacted with the D or A part of the ICT fluorophore, the charge distribution in the ICT system will be influenced and the molecular dipole property will be changed, inducing the change of absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum, reflected as optical spectrum red-shift or blue-shift (Chart 3 ). Usually, during the ICT process of a conjugated D-A fluorophore, the stereoscopic structure will also change and twist after excitation to form a charge transfer state, therefore, this kind of intermolecular ICT system is also called twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT).

One of the most attractive characteristics of CDs is their fascinating photoluminescence properties, no matter from the point of fundamental research or from technological applications. Compared with inorganic quantum dots, CDs are more suitable for fluorescent nanosensors owing to their low toxicity, good water solubility, high stability, and easy functionalization. Generally, three strategies are adopted for designing sensors based on photoluminescence: (1) direct interaction between CDs and an analyte; (2) combination of CDs and a specific acceptor which can selectively interact with a target analyte; and (3) integration of CDs with other substances as a whole sensing segment. In this section, CDs fluorescent sensors for different analytes are presented and the sensing mechanisms related are also inferred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent CDs-based sensors for metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Inappropriate levels of metal ions will bring severe damage to human body and environments. Researchers have developed numerous fluorescent sensors to detect metal ions including heavy metal ions. CDs-based probes are also synthesized to selectively recognize these ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Ag+ and Pb2+. Generally speaking, the fluorescence of CDs can be effectively quenched by these metal ions. Addition of stronger metal-chelating reagents, for example, ethylenediaminetetraacetate (EDTA) and S2‒, can recover the emission of CDs [21]. In recent years, bifunctional CDs are developed and the selectivity and sensitivity are greatly improved [22]. In the following contents, the sensing examples for some typical metal ions are selected to illustrate the sensing process with CDs.

Iron ion, is one of the most widely researched sensing ion using CDs. Iron deficiency is listed as the first case of micronutrient deficiencies. Too much intake or exposure to iron ion can also induce severe health problem. The effective modulation and monitoring of ferric ion in bio-system and environment is necessary. In fact, the first trial on many synthesized CDs was to study the fluorescence response in presence of ferric ion and at the same time, the quenching mechanism with ferric ion at different concentrations would be also discussed.

In order to further investigate the fluorescence quenching of CDs by Fe3+, Hu et al. synthesized CDs from m-phenylenediamine and ammonia in ethanol solution [29]. These emission colors of these CDs changed from blue, green to red, dependent on the CDs concentration and excitation wavelength. After Fe3+ addition at different concentrations, the fluorescence of blue-CDs was continuously quenched while the fluorescence of green-CDs continuously increased. It was regarded that the concentration dependence of PL behavior was related with the different interaction of CDs at different concentrations through IFE effect. The functional groups on the surface of CDs could combine with metal ions through coordination or chelating, promoting the electron transfer from PL center of CDs to the d orbital of metal ions. These diverse fluorescence properties of the CDs were utilized to selectively and sensitively distinguish Fe3+ as well as Cu2+ from other metal ions. The LOD value can be as low as ppm level. A more applicable CDs-based sensor for Fe3+ was reported by Prof. Kim. They just simply mixed cellulose pulp with CDs solution to prepare a fluorescent CDs-embedded cellulose thin film. The thin film had high photostability and could selectively response to Fe3+ through fluorescence quenching. The used thin film can be recycled after being washed with deionized water. However, the LOD still need to be improved [30].

Many other research groups also utilized above-mentioned energy transfer and PET to develop CDs-based sensors for Fe3+. CDs can possess different chemical functional groups on the surface dependent on the precursor sources and reaction conditions, and therefore, demonstrated different interaction properties with Fe3+for sensing. Table 1 summarizes representative CDs-based sensors for Fe3+ and their merits.

In addition to iron ions, copper ion is also an object for preferable sensing. As one of important heavy metal ion analytes, copper ion Cu2+ participates in many physiological processes including oxidative metabolism and normal development of tissues. For example, acting as a cofactor of polyphenol oxidase, Cu2+ can help plants resist invasion of exterior pathogenic bacteria. However, an excessive intake of Cu2+ will result in liver injury and even cause neurological diseases [40]. In human body, a high-level Cu2+ expression will decrease active oxygen content and induce some neurodegenerative diseases, e.g. Alzheimer's disease and Wilson's disease. In a water domain, even a lower level of Cu2+ can seriously affect the vitality of microorganisms. And an excessive quantity of Cu2+ will bring detrimental impact on the growth of aquatic animals and plants. Till now, most reported Cu2+ sensors are derived from organic molecule dye [41], and the synthesis is tedious, expensive, and sometimes toxic. Appearance of water-soluble CDs provides an efficient, inexpensive and environment-friendly route to the preparation of Cu2+ sensors.

Chen et al. [42] reported functional CDs modified with branched poly(ethyleneimine) (BPET-CDs) at an earlier time. The rich amine groups on the surface of BPEI-CDs can combine with cupric ion to form cupric amine. The absorption spectrum of cupric amine overlapped with excitation and emission spectra of BPET-CDs and therefore, the excitation and emission photon energy was absorbed by cupric amine, thus realizing fluorescence quenching through surficial IFE effect. The BPET-CDs sensor realized rapid, reliable, and selective detection of Cu2+ with an LOD as low as 6 nM and a dynamic detection range of 10‒1100 nM. The formed cupric amine would be dissociated with stronger cupric-chelating agent and the fluorescence of BPET-CDs could recover. Sun group combined the merits of ion imprinting polymers (IIP) and fluorescent CDs, and synthesized fluorescent sensors CQDs@Cu-IIP for Cu2+ detection [43]. For sensing, cupric ions can selectively interact with the amino groups to form a coordination bond, and are adsorbed by CQDs@Cu-IIP. The negatively charged CDs system became positively charged after complexation with cupric ion, and a PET process will occur. The fluorescence of CQDs@Cu-IIP could be effectively quenched even at a cupric ion concentration as low as 0.0016 mg/L. Finally, CQDs@Cu-IIP was also applied to the detection of cupric ions in real water samples with good recovery rates. Chiu et al. prepared fluorescent CDs from poly(vinylpyrrolidone) and l-cysteine through carbonization. Cupric ions interacted with the N and S atoms on the surface of CDs through complexation. The emission of CDs was effectively quenched owing to high metal-to-ligand binding affinity. Moreover, the CDs sensor has high endurance to ionic strength (up to 0.1 M NaCl) and pH value (2.0‒12.0), and showed high applicability to different occasions for cupric ion detection such as multicolor cell imaging [44]. Prof. Ni prepared adenine-stabilized CDs (A-CDs) and the fluorescence QY was 44.6% [45]. The A-CDs had high selectivity towards Cu2+ over other metal ions, anions, amino acids and biomolecules. Multiple characterizations were used to investigate the fluorescence quenching sensing mechanism. The high sensitivity and selectivity were ascribed to the Cu2+ coordination-induced aggregation of A-CDs, reduction of Cu2+ to Cu by adenine, and nonradiative PET process from A-CDs to Cu2+ or metallic Cu. The sensor showed a linear fluorescence response in 1‒700 nM concentration range and the LOD was as low as 0.3 nM. The sensor also had merits of good water solubility, high endurance to pH change, high photostability, fast response and low cytotoxicity.

It can be seen from above examples that the CDs sensor for Cu2+ generally contains N-derived groups including amino, ethylenimine, and aromatic N in order to better complex with Cu2+. As additional examples, Prof. Liu utilized electrospun carbon nanofibers to prepare N-doped CDs. The strong interaction between Cu2+ ions and surface functional groups of the CDs induced the fluorescence quenching after Cu2+ addition. The fluorescent platform showed high sensitivity and selectivity to Cu2+ ions with a detection limit of 5 nM in the linear range of 0‒10 μM [46]. Chen et al. prepared N,B-codoped CDs through combustion process. The green-emissive CDs had high resistance to photo bleaching and ionic strength and could response to Cu2+ via static fluoresce quenching. The linear range for detection was 1 μM to 25 μM and the LOD was 0.3 μM [47]. Sometimes, other heteroatoms such as S and O were needed to be incorporated into CDs. Additional representative CDs examples for Cu2+ sensing including the precursors, preparation route, and LOD values were listed in following Table 2 for reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-based sensors for Hg2+ and Ag+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: As two representatives of heavy metal ions, Hg2+ and Ag+ are known more due to their notorious negative influence on waters, atmosphere, crops, fishery products, and human body. These two kinds of metal ions exert effect through binding with negatively charged groups on proteins such as mercapto. Mercury is a persistent bio-accumulative toxic metal. The metabolism of Hg2+ in human body is very slow. Hg2+ can also enter the brain across the blood-brain barrier, and induce permanent damage to central nervous system and cerebral asthenia syndrome. Ag+ can inactivate various metabolites and sulfhydryl enzymes by interacting with thiol, amino, and carboxyl groups to produce cytopathogenic effects, although nano-Ag and Ag+ have been used for a long time owing to their antibacterial activity. The high-sensitive detection of Hg2+ and Ag+ levels in environments and biological samples is of vital importance.

Jiang Group reported the preparation of blue-emissive high-quantum-yield CDs from sodium citrate [58]. The use of ammonium bicarbonate is crucial to the formation of fluorescent CDs. There were C, N, O and Na elements on the CDs surface. The chelation of Hg2+ with the carboxylate and hydroxyl groups makes the CDs close to each other so as to promote the nonradiative recombination of excitons through electron transfer process. Moreover, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs showed blue-shift after Hg2+ addition, implying that the addition of Hg2+ changed the electronic structure of CDs. The LOD was as low as 10 nM and the linear range was 0‒5 μM. Finally, real water samples were tested with high reliability. The CDs-based Hg2+ sensor has a great potential application. In the study from Yan group [59], both two kinds of fluorescent CDs prepared from different precursors can response to Hg2+. The fluorescence quenching is caused by the complexation between Hg2+ and carboxyl groups. It was also found that the introduction of heteroatom would improve fluorescence efficiency and the presence of electron-rich amino groups was favorable for sensitive fluorescence quenching. The interference from Fe3+ was also excluded. Combining the studies from Jiang and Yan, we can draw a straightforward deduction that, in designing CDs-based sensor for Hg2+, the introduction of hydroxyl and carboxyl (carboxylate), especially carboxyl (carboxylate) group is necessary. Appropriate pH value can help deprotonation of carboxylic group and bonding between Hg2+ and CDs will be enhanced, thus improving quenching efficiency. Higher pH conditions are not favorable and Hg2+ would prefer to combine with hydroxylate ion instead of CDs. The complexation of Hg2+ with acetate ion is much stronger than other metal ions. These clues provide guidance to the design of efficient CDs-based Hg2+ sensors. Following this instruction, Tadesse prepared N-doped CDs possessing higher QY (∼31%) and high photobleaching resistance [60]. The N- and O-containing groups of these NCDs formed coordination bond with Hg2+ so as to induce fluorescence quenching through favored nonradiative electron/hole recombination/annihilation. The linear range of detection was between 0.001‒1 μM and LOD was as low as 5.3 nM with high precision. Finally, real water sample monitoring and multicolor cell imaging were carried out using the NCDs with acceptable results. Recently, Causse reported solid-state fluorescent CDs [61]. They prepared fluorescent test strip using nylon and the CDs sensor for fluorimetric sensing of Hg2+ with on-off mode. The diverse speciation of nitrogen (graphitic N, pyridinic N, pyrrolic N and amino N) and disorder in graphitic carbon core accounted for the solid-state yellow luminescence. This is one of few examples about solid-emissive CDs for sensing application [62].

Lin et al. [63] reported yellow-emissive CDs (y-CDs) that were prepared from 1,2,4-triaminobenzene carbon source for bifunctional sensing of Ag+ and cysteine. The fluorescence quenching of y-CDs caused by Ag+ was ascribed to the chelation of Ag+ with y-CDs, triggering the PET process from excited state of y-CDs to Ag+. The stronger binding between Ag+ and cysteine can again restore the fluorescence of y-CDs (Fig. 2 ). The interference of Hg2+ can be avoided through addition of masking agent sulfocarbamide. Green-emissive CDs synthesized from 4-bormoaniline and ethylenediamine showed high QY and excellent biocompatibility, and could also be used for consecutive sensing of Ag+ and cysteine through similar photophysical process [64]. The LOD values were 3.9 μM (Ag+) and 3.4 μM (Cys), respectively. Orange-emissive CDs prepared from p-aminobenzoic acid and p-aminophenol can also selectively response to Ag+ in water samples and human serum sample, showing valuable prospects in environmental protection and biological assays [65]. Prof. Tang synthesized nitrogen-rich CDs utilizing 2-azidoimdazole with glycerol crosslinker through microwave-assisted hydrothermal process. The prepared CDs with high QY (27.9%) were employed as an effective chemosensor for Ag+ detection via static quenching effect [66]. The CDs sensor had a good linear range of Ag+ detection from 20 nM to 6 μM and an LOD value of 6.3 nM. The interference from Hg2+ and Cu2+ could well be excluded using masking agent EDTA while other metal ions has almost no influence. Furthermore, the CDs materials could also be used as a turn-on fluorescence sensor for biothiols such as cysteine, homocysteine, and glutathione based on the NCDs/Ag+ complex.

Till now, most CDs-based chemosensors for metal ions have good sensitivity and specificity. Owing to the natural water-soluble property and fluorescence of CDs in solution state, these sensors can be easily applied to metal ion detection in water samples and biological systems. Various kinds of CDs are synthesized for sensing the metal ion species, not limited to the metal ions mentioned above in the paper. The performance of CDs sensors still needs improvement from multiple aspects such as longer-wavelength fluorescence, turn-on sensing, higher fluorescence QY, long-term stability, and higher interference resistance. There is still a large space to perfect the performance of CDs sensors for metal ion detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent CDs-based sensors for bio-active small molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Some important small molecules, such as volatile organic compounds, explosive chemicals, pesticides, antioxidant, and biological reagents also put forwards severe threats to human body, environment, water quality and ecological persistence while bringing significant economical profits. Therefore, the proper utilization and posttreatment of these small molecules are of significance. The detection of these small molecules is the first step towards this target. Fluorescent CDs are also designed and synthesized to detect theses small molecules. There is a vast literature on sensing of various small molecules. In Sections 3.2 and 3.3, some related studies for biological small molecule glutathione and nitroaromatic explosives in recent years are outlined as representatives.

Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide containing γ-amido and thiol groups. GSH acts as antioxidant in plants, animals, fungi, and some bacteria and archaea and can prevent damage to cells caused by reactive oxygen species. GSH formulations are used clinically for detoxification, senescence delaying, and inflammation treatment, etc. GSH also shows effect on human immunodeficiency virus (HIV), Parkinson disease, Alzheimer disease, autism, cystic fibrosis, and cardiovascular diseases [67].

Chemosensors based on CDs for GSH were also developed. Among the three kinds of CDs prepared from o-phenylenediamine (o-CDs), m-phenylenediamine (m-CDs) and p-phenylenediamine (p-CDs) in DMF solutions, only the red-emissive p-CDs can sensitively response to GSH [68]. Furthermore, p-CDs can fast, selectively, visually, and accurately detect GSH over cysteine, homocysteine, amino acid and ions by colorimetric and fluorescence dual modes. The LOD is 0.7 μM, much lower than the level of cellular GSH. It was regarded that the higher content of pyridinic N in p-CDs was responsible for the selective fluorescence quenching of p-CDs upon GSH. Ma et al. reported B-doped CDs with fluorescence QY 42% from citric acid and sodium tetraphenylborate precursors [69]. The boron hydroxyl structure ‒B(OH)2 groups on B-CDs can bond with ortho hydroxyl groups of catechol to form a stable borate complex, triggering PET process and inducing fluorescence quenching of B-CDs. Following addition of GSH will dissociate the borate complex to initiate a derivatization reaction and recover the fluorescence of B-CDs. The off-on sensor for catechol and GSH has excellent detection performance with a linear range of 1‒50 nM and an LOD of 0.25 nM for catethol, and for sensing GSH with a linear range of 2‒100 nM and an LOD of 0.5 nM. Liang et al. prepared N-doped CDs (NCDs) from fulvic acid, urea and citric acid through microwaving pyrolysis [22b]. Density functional theory (DFT) and frontier molecular orbital (FMO) calculations indicated that the synthesized NCDs exhibited stronger interaction with Fe3+ over other metal ions (Cr3+, Co2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ba2+ and Pb2+) and would prefer to adsorb Fe3+ on the surface and form a stable complex. And then a PET process would occur and the fluorescence of NCDs would be quenched. The addition of reducing agent GSH could reduce Fe3+ into Fe2+, PET process would be inhibited and the fluorescence could recover. This on-off-on bifunctional fluorescent sensor showed great potential in practical S. thermophilus strain screening. In recent years, many research groups have reported bifunctional CDs-based on-off-on sensors for simultaneous detection of metal ions and GSH. Generally, the fluorescence of CDs was first quenched by metal ions and would recover after GSH addition. These kinds of sensors are outlined in Table 3 for reference.

Researchers also prepared a series of CDs sensors for another important biomolecule, dopamine (DA). DA is an important catecholamine neurotransmitter and plays a key role in the normal functioning of renal, cardiovascular, and central nervous systems. The abnormal level of DA is a symptom of neurological disorders, expressed by dementia, Parkinson's disease, schizophrenia, etc. [76]. Chung et al. [77] prepared water-soluble N-doped CDs (NCDs) from citric acid and urea, and then the NCDs were passivated with GSH to enhance the emission further. Lifetime and optical spectra measurement indicated that the PET process and IFE effect accounted for the DA-induced quenching of the GSHNCDs fluorescence. Therein, the hydrogen bonding between DA and GSH on surface of GSHNCDs took important role in the energy transfer. The LOD was as low as 1.01 nM with a linear detection range of 20 nM to 10 μM. Fan group synthesized N-doped CDs (NCDs) from glycine and urea through a simple solid-heat method [78]. The fluorescence of NCDs can be selectively quenched by DA in aqueous solution. The LOD was 0.0972 μM. It was concluded that the excited-state energy transfer of NCDs to existing dopamine quinone was the main reason for fluorescence quenching. Additionally, this NCDs sensor was successfully used to determination of DA in human serum and rat serum with satisfactory results. Park et al. synthesized phenylboronic acid-functionalized CDs (rBA-CDs), which was then solidified on an acrylate-functionalized glass substrate for DA sensing [79]. The phenylboronic acid group of rBA-CDs could selectively react with DA to form a new fluorescent species, whose emission peak was located at longer wavelength. The higher the DA concentration was, the higher the fluorescence intensity would be. Furthermore, this sensor was also applied to the detection of DA in human serum spiked with DA with a good recovery rate. This is one example of few solid-film CDs-based sensors. However, the specific sensing mechanism using rBA-CDs for DA was not elaborated in detail in this article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs-based sensors for glucose and other small bioactive molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Glucose is the most predominant and important monosaccharide found in nature. It can fast provide energy for cell and is a necessary nutrient for biological metabolism. However, high-level blood glucose will increase insulin concentration and induce obesity and diabetes, and low glucose level will bring hypoglycemia, even more serious insulin shock. Furthermore, glucose plays an important role in memory enhancement and calcium absorption. Glucose is also widely used in food production and medicine processing. The appropriate content of glucose in biological body and proper addition of glucose in food and medicine are necessary. Therefore, the detection of glucose also becomes essential and crucial.

Zhang et al. prepared fluorescent CDs using ethylene glycol, citric acid and histidine through microwave-assisted polyol method [80]. The emission wavelength of the CDs can be tuned from 430 nm to 548 nm by changing the precursors' ratio. Enzyme Con A-coupled CDs were homogeneously mixed with chitosan-linked graphene oxide (GO) fluorescence quencher. The homogeneous sensing system can specifically combine with glucose, after which GO would be detached from the CDs and the fluorescence of CDs recovered. This sensing system was successfully applied to the detection of glucose in blood and tear within a large detection concentration. Huang group provided another similar turn-on CDs-based sensor for glucose [81]. A CDs/MnO2 assembly can be obtained by mixing CDs and MnO2 nanosheet. The fluorescence of CDs would be quenched due to FRET process. H2O2 generated during oxidation of glucose by glucose oxidase can oxidize the MnO2 nanosheet, the assembly would be destroyed, and the fluorescence of CDs would be recovered, thus realized detection of glucose. Although the LOD and application range of the sensor were acceptable, the pretreatment time seemed a little longer (at least 40 min). Earlier study from Qin group reported similar result [82a]. In their work, MnO2 nanosheet in-situ grew on the surface of CDs after reduction of KMnO4. During the process of oxidation of glucose into gluconic acid in the presence of glucose oxidase, H2O2 is produced and can reduce the oxidizing species MnO2 assembled onto CDs, and the quenched fluorescence of CDs can be recovered. The detection limit was as low as 44 nM. This strategy is often used in the design of CDs-based sensor for glucose and metal ions [82b,82c].

Besides above mentioned biological small molecules, CDs-based sensing systems for other bioactive drugs have also been extensively developed, such as levodopa, doxycycline, tetracycline, and doxorubicin. The CDs sensor and their sensing performance for these small molecules are cited as instances in Table 4 .

Nitroaromatic compounds are most commonly used as explosives. The improper storage and abuse of explosives are a huge threat to life and treasures. Probing nitroaromatic compounds at earlier stage of chemical explosion is of great importance [90].

Researchers developed chemical sensors based on CDs to detect this kind of explosives. Zhang et al. synthesized yellow-green CDs with low toxicity and high photostability. This CDs sensor can selectively and precisely determine 2,4,6-trinitrophenol from other explosives including 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,4,6-trinitro-1,3,5-triaminobenzne, Hexogen and Octogen. It was regarded that hydrogen-bond interaction-assisted energy transfer and proton transfer-assisted energy transfer should be responsible for sensing process (Fig. 3 ). Finally, visual detection of TNP was successfully performed using indicator paper with satisfactory result [91]. Algarra et al. utilized acidic oxidation of activated carbon and post-functionalization with polyamidoamine generation-4 dendrimer (PAMAM-NH2) to prepare electron-enriched CDs [92]. The CDs sensor could selectively response to 4‑chloro-2,6-dinitroaniline other than nitroaromatic compounds else. After interaction with 4‑chloro-2,6-dinitroaniline, the fluorescence intensity of CDs would be decreased and a new peak at longer emission wavelength would appear. It was postulated that the new peak was related with charge transfer and the formation of Meisenheimer complex. The linear detection ranged from 1.0 × 10–5‒6.0 × 10–4 M with a detection limit of 2 μM. Similar phenomenon and sensing mechanism were also discovered and explored by Su group for detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol using CDs, with the exception that the new peak appeared at shorter emission wavelength [93]. These are two examples of few ratiometric nanosensors for explosive detection. In 2019, Su also reported a turn-on N,S-codoped CDs sensor for detection of nitrophenols (2-nitrophenol, 3-nitrophenol and 4-nitrophenol) from other nitro compounds including TNT [94]. The LODs for the three nitrophenols were low to 39 nM, 43 nM and 26 nM, respectively. Electron transfer from the amino groups of the N,S-codoped CDs to the nitro groups of nitrophenols was the reason for fluorescence quenching. However, this sensor could not distinguish the three isomers from each other.

Yang et al. [95] synthesized fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) via microwave digestion of glucosamine and ethylenediamine precursors. The fluorescence of NCDs would be quenched after addition of 4-nitrophenol or Cr2O7 2‒ ion. Optical spectra showed that the absorption spectra of 4-nitrophenol and Cr2O7 2‒ were partially overlapped with the excitation spectrum of NCDs, and the quenching mechanism should be IFE effect. Once reduction of Cr2O7 2‒ into Cr(III) by ascorbic acid, the IFE effect was weakened and the fluorescence would restore. Finally, the sensor was applied to the detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid in river and lake water with acceptable precision. Lü group [96] protected the red-emitting CDs (prepared from p-phenylenediamine) with a mussel nanocoating of polydopamine (PDA) so as to improve the stability of the fluorescent system. The fluorescence of CDs@PDA would response to 4-nitrophenol and Fe3+. The absorption spectrum of 4-nitrophenol significantly overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs. Therefore, the IFE effect should be responsible for the fluorescence quenching. For detection of metal ions, only Fe3+ can simultaneously induce the fluorescence decrease at UV and red regions, which was the main contribution in distinguishing Fe3+ from other interfering Cu2+, Pb2+ and Al3+ ions. However, almost few CDs sensors are reported for detecting explosives at gaseous state, although various CDs-based sensors are developed for sensing explosives with low detection limit and high sensitivity.

Through twenty years of study and development, various CDs-based sensors have been devised for sensing multifarious analytes. For a specific analyte, a well-direct process is needed for surface modification of CDs, so as to incorporate a corresponding recognizing part. Owing to the intrinsic and rich electron-donating groups, it is easier for CDs to be applied in sensing electron-deficient analytes including metal ions, electron-accepting nitroaromatic compounds, easy-to-be-oxidized phenols, and glucose. For application in electron-donating analytes, surface functionalization of CDs is necessary. Till now, CDs-based sensors have exhibited excellent performance regarding sensitivity, selectivity, LOD, linear range, etc. In our opinion, for the technological application of CDs in fluorescent sensing, more attention should be payed to the following aspects: (1) accurate structure identification of CDs, especially the recognizing part on the surface of CDs, which is crucial to sensor design, (2) design and synthesis of solid-emissive CDs, which is more favorable for practical application, and (3) design and synthesis of CDs applicable to sensing of gaseous analytes, which is of great importance to detect explosives and toxic gases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52073082, 51603063 and 22073025) and Overseas Expertise Introduction Project for Discipline Innovation (D18025). We also thanked Prof. Yanyan Fu of Shanghai Institute of Microsystem and Information Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, for constructible discussion on the sensing mechanism.
